fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Let H \colon f \sim g be a homotopy between two functions f,g\colon A \to B. For any identification p\colon x =y in A, we have an identification H x \cdot \mathsf {act}_{g}\,p = \mathsf {act}_{f}\,p\cdot Hy.
By identification induction on p, it suffices to show that Hx\cdot \mathsf {refl} = \mathsf {refl}\cdot Hx.

fr:title.text: Naturality of homotopies onto the identity

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Naturality of homotopies onto the identity

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Let H \colon f \sim 1_{A} be a homotopy from f\colon A \to A onto the identity function. Then we may construct a higher homotopy from H\circ f to \mathsf {act}_{f}\circ H.
The fiber of a function f\colon A \to B at an element b:B is the type consisting of elements a\colon A equipped with identifications p\colon fa=b.
When s\colon A\to B is a section of r\colon B\to A, we say that A is a retract of B.
A biinverse to a function f\colon A \to B is defined to be a pair of a section and a retraction to f. We will refer to a function f\colon A\to B equipped with a biinverse as a biinvertible function.

A quasi-inverse to a function f \colon A \to B is a function g \colon B \to A equipped with homotopies \epsilon \colon f \circ g \sim 1_{B} and \eta \colon g \circ f \sim 1_{A}, rendering g simultaneously a section and a retraction of f.
Although no coherence laws are assumed for \eta and \epsilon , we shall refer to them respectively as the unit and counit of the quasi-inverse.

An inverse to a function f\colon A \to B is defined to be a quasi-inverse equipped with an additional higher coherence \chi \colon \mathsf {act}_{f}\circ \eta \sim \epsilon \circ f in f\circ g \circ f \sim f relating the (a priori incoherent) unit and the counit of the quasi-inverse. We will refer to a function f\colon A\to B equipped with an inverse as an invertible function.
A function f\colon A\to B is called an embedding when for any x,y:A the action on identifications \mathsf {act}_{f}\colon x=y\to fx=fy is invertible.
An equivalence from A to B is defined to be a function A \to B equipped with a biinverse.

Let H \colon f \sim g be a homotopy between two functions f,g\colon A \to B. For any identification p\colon x =y in A, we have an identification H x \cdot \mathsf {act}_{g}\,p = \mathsf {act}_{f}\,p\cdot Hy.
By identification induction on p, it suffices to show that Hx\cdot \mathsf {refl} = \mathsf {refl}\cdot Hx.

fr:title.text: Naturality of homotopies onto the identity

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Naturality of homotopies onto the identity

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Let H \colon f \sim 1_{A} be a homotopy from f\colon A \to A onto the identity function. Then we may construct a higher homotopy from H\circ f to \mathsf {act}_{f}\circ H.
Any quasi-invertible map f\colon A\to B is biinvertible.
The quasi-inverse to f is simultaneously a section and a retraction.

Let f \colon A \to B have a quasi-inverse g\colon B\to A with incoherent counit \epsilon \colon f\circ g\sim 1_{B} and unit \eta \colon g\circ f\sim 1_{A}. We can correct the counit to obtain a coherent inverse to f\colon A\to B.
The corrected counit \epsilon ' \colon g\circ f\sim 1_{A} is defined like so:
The coherence \chi \colon \mathsf {act}_{f}\circ \eta \sim \epsilon '\circ f can be constructed using our two naturality lemmas Lemma and Lemma .

Let f\colon A\to B be biinvertible with s,r\colon B\to A and h_s\colon f \circ s \sim 1_{B} and h_r\colon r\circ f = 1_{A}. Then we may define a homotopy h' \colon s\circ f \sim 1_{A} exhibiting s\colon B\to A as a retraction of f\colon A\to B. Therefore, we may define a quasi-inverse to f tracked by s\colon B\to A.
We have s\circ f \sim r\circ f \circ s\circ f by h_r and r\circ f \circ s\circ f\sim r \circ f by h_s and r\circ f \sim 1_{A} by h_r.
Let f\colon A \to B and g\colon B\to C be maps equipped with sections s_f\colon B\to A and s_g\colon C\to B. The composite s_f\circ s_g\colon C\to A is a section of g\circ f\colon A \to C.
The witness of sectionhood is defined using the witnesses for the two sections.
Let f\colon A \to B and g\colon B\to C be maps equipped with retractions r_f\colon B\to A and r_g\colon C\to B. The composite r_f\circ r_g\colon C\to A is a retraction of g\circ f\colon A \to C.
The witness of retractionhood is defined using the witnesses for the two retractions.

Let f\colon A\to B and g\colon B\to C be biinvertible maps. Then the composite g\circ f\colon A \to C is biinvertible, with sections and retractions given by composites of the component sections and retractions.

Let f\colon A\to B and g\colon B\to C be quasi-invertible maps. Then the composite g\circ f\colon A \to C is quasi-invertible, with sectionhood and retractionhood witnesses given by those of the composites of the component sections and retractions.
Let f\colon A\to B and g\colon B\to C be invertible maps. Then the composite g\circ f\colon A\to C is invertible.
By Lemma , it suffices to find a quasi-inverse to the composite g\circ f; we take this to be the composite (Construction ) of the underlying quasi-inverses of f and g.
Let s\colon A\to B exhibit A as a retract of B; then \mathsf {act}_{s}\colon x = y\to sx=sy exhibits x=y as a retract of sx=sy for each x,y:A.
